title: Dark malt loaf
subtitle: The dense, sticky loaf made with treacle and dark malt extract
excerpt: A dark, sticky, dense loaf baked in a tin — the kind that slices thinly and eats best cold with butter. Black treacle, dark malt extract, and mixed dried fruit. No kneading required.
sourceNotes: Dark malt loaf is a British bakery staple with documented roots in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The sticky loaf format, relying on treacle and malt extract for colour, sweetness, and texture, appears in British breadmaking guides of the 1920s and 1930s. The low-yeast, long-rest formula here follows the traditional commercial bakery approach adapted for domestic ovens.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice cold and freeze flat. Eats well toasted from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The loaf improves over 24 to 48 hours; bake the day before and wrap tightly.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, low and slow. Fan at 140°C.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, very dark syrup, a by-product of sugar refining. Stronger in flavour than golden syrup, with a slightly bitter, robust sweetness. Used in malt loaf, parkin, and gingerbread to add colour and depth. Not the same as US molasses, which is less refined and has a different flavour profile.

content.text: Dark malt loaf is one of the simpler yeast breads to make at home. The batter-like dough requires no kneading: it is too wet to knead. The long, low bake produces the characteristic dense, slightly springy crumb.
is essential; do not substitute golden syrup, which produces a milder and less characteristic result.

Combine both flours, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Dissolve the malt extract and treacle in the lukewarm water (the easiest way is to dip the measuring spoon in the water first, then the syrup slides off cleanly). Add the liquid to the flour and stir vigorously until a smooth, very wet batter forms. Fold in the dried fruit. The mixture should look more like a thick cake batter than a bread dough; this is correct.
Cover the bowl and leave at room temperature for 1 hour. The batter will bubble and rise noticeably but will not double like a conventional dough. Grease the loaf tin thoroughly.

Preheat the oven to 160°C conventional. Scrape the batter into the greased tin and smooth the top. Bake for 55 to 65 minutes; the loaf is done when a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean (a few sticky fruit fragments on the skewer are fine). The top should be deep brown, not black; if it darkens too quickly cover loosely with foil from 30 minutes.

While the loaf is still warm, brush the top with a thin glaze of malt extract diluted with a little warm water (about 1 tsp malt to 2 tsp water). This gives the characteristic sticky, shiny crust. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then turn out and cool completely on a rack. The loaf is better the day after baking; wrap it tightly in foil once cool.
